description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has been paged because an application
  has suddenly started returning a large number of HTTP 500 errors.  
  A single log file has already been copied to the machine for you:

      /home/user/app/logs/access.log

  1. From this file you must create a concise “status‐code frequency” report and
     save it as

      /home/user/app/reports/status_code_frequency.txt

     • Each line of the report must have the exact format

           <STATUS_CODE>: <COUNT>

       where  
         – <STATUS_CODE> is the three–digit HTTP status code  
         – <COUNT>     is how many times that status code appears in the log  
       • Sort the lines primarily by <COUNT> in **numeric descending** order.  
       • If two status codes have the same count, order those two lines
         by the status code value in **ascending** numeric order.  
       • The report must contain one line per distinct status code and must end
         with a single trailing newline.

     Example of the required syntax (numbers are illustrative only):

         200: 157
         404: 23
         500: 3⏎          ← there must be an actual newline after the final line

  2. Next, you have to identify which client IP addresses are responsible
     for the 500 errors and list the **top three** offenders.  
     Produce a second report file

      /home/user/app/reports/top_500_ips.txt

     • Each line must have the exact format

           <IP_ADDRESS> <COUNT>

       where  
         – <IP_ADDRESS> is the offending client’s IP  
         – <COUNT>      is the number of 500-response lines produced by that IP  
       • Only include the three IPs with the highest counts.  
       • Sort the lines primarily by <COUNT> in **numeric descending** order.  
       • If two IPs share the same count, order those lines in normal
         lexicographical (ASCII) ascending order.  
       • Terminate the file with a single trailing newline.

     Example layout (again, numbers are illustrative):

         10.0.0.12 7
         192.168.3.4 7
         172.20.1.55 5⏎

  3. When the two report files have been created, display the contents of
     /home/user/app/reports/status_code_frequency.txt to the terminal so that
     a human can quickly see the distribution.

  You have standard GNU/Linux utilities (grep, awk, cut, sort, uniq, head,
  etc.) available but **no sudo**.  
  Everything you need is already inside /home/user/app; do **not** download
  anything from the Internet.

  Completing the above steps correctly should take no more than 5-10 shell
  commands.  Good luck!
name: task_000000_fc990ec3
